images.caption: Figure 2:Two experts vs. one generalist: (a) The prevailing monolithic architecture employs a shared backbone with task-specific heads to jointly predict camera poses and 3DGS representations in a single forward pass. (b) The two-expert architecture explicitly decomposes the process into sequential stages: a geometry expert first estimates camera poses from multi-view input images, and a dedicated appearance (posed feed-forward 3DGS) expert subsequently generates 3D Gaussian representations conditioned on the predicted poses and input images.
Figure 3:Qualitative comparison on DL3DV with varying numbers of input views.
Figure 6:Qualitative cross-dataset generalization from DL3DV to ScanNet++.

Figure 7:PSNR vs. inference speed on DL3DV (224×224224\times 224resolution). 6, 12, and 24 input views from left to right. The circle sizes are proportional to the model sizes, and the numbers inside indicate the number of parameters.
Figure 8:Qualitative comparison on DL3DV (224×224224\times 224resolution, 1-3 rows: 6 context views, 4-6 rows: 12 context views, 7-9 rows: 24 context views).
Figure 9:Qualitative comparison on RE10K dataset with 6 context views.

Figure 10:Qualitative cross-dataset generalization results from DL3DV to ScanNet++ (224×224224\times 224resolution, 1-2 rows: 32 context views, 3-4 rows: 64 context views, 5-6 rows: 128 context views).
Figure 11:Qualitative comparison on high-resolution DL3DV (960×540960\times 540, 1-2 rows: 16 context views, 3-4 rows: 32 context views, 5-6 rows: 64 context views, 7-8 rows: 128 context views).
